yttrium and lead.

Main reference.
Graphs of preliminary data
for iron and yttrium.
Exp. details, data analysis.
First author - Angelica
Hildebrand (now her family name is Oehrn).
Graphs only for C-12, Pb-208, Y-89 (prelim.)
Details of neutron
beam facility and SCANDAL setup at Swedberg Lab.,
Uppsala, Sweden.
Exp.neutron spectra
for Li-7(p,n)  reaction, used in data analysis.
Monte Carlo code,
later  updates by N.Olsson.
http://mcnp-gree.lanl.gov/index.html - L.S.Waters+,
MCNPX Users' Manual, version 2.1.5. LANL, 14 Nov.1999.
SCANDAL - Scattered Neutron Detection Assembly.
Li-7(p,n)reaction. Li-7 isotopically enriched
System of three collimators defined a 9cm diameter
neutron beam at scattering target, typical neutron
yield was  2.5E+6 1/sec over whole beam area.
Neutron energy was monitoring by TFBC and by
integrated proton beam current from proton beam dump.
Resulting neutron spectrum consisted of 96+-0.5 MeV
peak and low-energy tail which was suppressed by
time-of-flight techniques.
Charge particles in neutron beam < 1%.
Detection of neutrons was based on conversion to
protons and detection of recoil protons.
Background was defined by removing the scattered
cylinder from setup.
Protons were separated from other charged
particles by deltaE - E technique.
To reject events from low-energy tail of neutron
spectrum, time-of-flight cut was used.
Each of two arms (for 10-50 deg and 30-70 deg)
has 2mm hick veto scintillator for fast charged
particle rejection,
10 mm thick neutron-proton converter scintillator,
2mm thick deltaE plastic scintillator (also as part of
trigger),
array of 12 CsI detectors for energy
determination of recoil protons,
two trigger scintillators,
two drift chambers.
Fission counter TFBC for number of neutrons in
beam. TFBC uncertainty more 10 %.
Total energy resolution of individual CsI crystals is
different, on average 3.7 MeV (FWHM).
Dead time in data acquisition system was about
for iron - 14%, for yttrium - 6%, for lead - 4%,
for background - 2%.
Total drift chamber efficiency was measured to  be
Total proton detection efficiency 0.64 +- 0.10.
C-H2 sample serves for calibration purposes.
Four scattering targets were used:
Iron cylinder- 91.8% Fe-56, 5.8% Fe-54, 2.1% Fe-57,
0.3% Fe-58, 5 cm high, 5 cm diameter, 777g mass,
Yttrium cylinder- 5.2cm high, 5cm diameter, 456g mass,
Radiogenic lead cylinder- 88% Pb-208, 11% Pb-206,
1% Pb-207, 6.3 cm high,2.9 cm diameter, 444 g mass,
Carbon cylinder - 5 cm high, 5 cm diameter, 178g mass
(to provide data for normalization).
Background data were subtracted from signal
spectra after normalization to the same neutron
fluence and taking dead time into consideration.
Spectra were fitted by Gaussians to ground state peak
and lowest excited state. For Fe-56 Gaussians was
fitted to excited state at 4.5 MeV, for Y-89 - to
states at 4.0, 7.0, 9.0 MeV.
For large angles ground state yield was extracted by
integration with limits determined visually.
-for Fe and Y.
For low-energy continuum originating from Li-7(p,n)
reaction - typically around 3%, always < 6%.
For background and dead time.
For neutron attenuation and multiple scattering by
Monte Carlo code for Fe and Y, by MCNPX code and data
of ENDF/B for Pb.
Total error includes statistical, background
subtraction error, error due to correction for multiple
scattering.
Relative statistical errors(before corrections)
Error due to angle uncertainty
Error due to correction for contribution from
low-energy continuum of Li-7(p,n) reaction - 0.6 %,
assuming  relative uncertainty of 10 % in correction.
Position of target and drift chambers are known
slightly better than 1 mm, resulting in angular
uncertainty of about 1 deg.
Imperfect calibration can produce conversion position
errors up to about 0.5 mm, which corresponds to about
0.5 deg shift of presumed angle; this uncertainty was
randomly distributed among data points.
Phenomenological and microscopic optical model
predictions. Study of deviation from Wick's limit.
M.M.

Two authors names were added.
M.M. Monitor was moved starting in 12 col.
